claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. 

To start, I need to gather some information about your circumstances and your children. Let me begin with a few questions.

**What is the name of your child (or first child, if you have more than one)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible. 

My child's name is Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Let me ask you a few more questions to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

**How old is Alex, or what is Alex's date of birth?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful information.

**Is Alex currently living with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good, thank you. Before I can make a determination, I need to check the specific eligibility criteria. Let me review the official rules.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I've reviewed the eligibility criteria. I need to ask a few more questions to complete my assessment.

**Are you a British citizen, or do you have a right to reside in the UK (for example, settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or are you from an EEA or Swiss national family)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and I'm a British citizen.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful.

**Are you currently responsible for Alex - meaning do you live with Alex and/or are you contributing towards their care (such as food, clothes, or pocket money)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for Alex. They live with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good. I have a few more questions to complete my assessment.

**Is Alex currently in hospital, care, or any form of residential accommodation?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, yes - Alex has been in hospital for a few months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. I need to gather a bit more detail about this situation.

**How many weeks has Alex been in hospital for? Is it more or less than 12 weeks?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask one more important question to complete my assessment.

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while they are in hospital (for example, on visits, extras, or other expenses related to their care)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy their clothes, magazines, and favorite food while they're in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now carefully review the eligibility criteria against your circumstances.

Based on the official rules I've reviewed:

**Key eligibility factors:**
1. You are a British citizen living in the UK ✓
2. You are responsible for Alex (they live with you and you contribute to their care) ✓
3. Alex is 8 years old (under 16) ✓
4. Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (more than 12 weeks)

According to the Child Benefit rules for children in hospital: Usually, you're no longer entitled to Child Benefit after 12 weeks in hospital. However, **there is an important exception**: if you regularly spend money on the child while they are in hospital, you can continue to receive Child Benefit.

Since you have confirmed that you are regularly spending money on Alex (clothes, magazines, food, etc.) while they are in hospital, this exception applies to you.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!**

Your eligibility is based on the exception within the hospital care rules. Although Alex has now been in hospital for longer than the standard 12-week limit, because you are regularly spending money on Alex's upkeep while in hospital (clothes, magazines, food), you can continue to receive Child Benefit.

If you haven't already claimed, you can apply for Child Benefit now. If your circumstances change or Alex is discharged from hospital, make sure to notify HMRC so they have the most up-to-date information about your situation.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), and you are a British citizen living in the UK with responsibility for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the standard 12-week limit for hospital stays), the eligibility rules contain an exception: you can continue to receive Child Benefit for children in hospital if you regularly spend money on them. You have confirmed that you regularly contribute financially to Alex's care in hospital (clothes, magazines, food), which meets this exception criteria. Therefore, you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex's 15-week hospital stay would normally result in loss of eligibility after 12 weeks, your regular financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep while in hospital allow you to continue receiving Child Benefit under the established exception to the 12-week rule."

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), and you are a British citizen living in the UK with responsibility for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the standard 12-week limit for hospital stays), the eligibility rules contain an exception: you can continue to receive Child Benefit for children in hospital if you regularly spend money on them. You have confirmed that you regularly contribute financially to Alex's care in hospital (clothes, magazines, food), which meets this exception criteria. Therefore, you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex's 15-week hospital stay would normally result in loss of eligibility after 12 weeks, your regular financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep while in hospital allow you to continue receiving Child Benefit under the established exception to the 12-week rule."
